title: Matroid Polytopes, Nested Sets and Bergman Fans
overall_summary: The paper gives a polyhedral and combinatorial construction of the Bergman fan of a matroid as a subfan of the normal fan of the matroid polytope. Its central result is that, for every building set in the lattice of flats, the associated nested set fan is a unimodular triangulation of the Bergman fan; locally at a basis this is proved by identifying the local Bergman fan with the normal fan of a Minkowski sum of simplices. The paper also identifies the face of the matroid polytope associated to a flat as a product/direct sum of a restriction and a contraction, and characterizes facet-defining flats by connectedness of these two minors. For the original problem, the most useful extracted tools are the explicit control of Trop(M) via flats, contractions, interval minors, and nested-set decompositions, especially Theorem 4.4, which decomposes initial matroids on nested cones as direct sums of matroids attached to intervals in the lattice of flats.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank r matroid on ground set [n] = {1,...,n}, with set of bases also denoted M. For a basis σ write e_σ = sum_{i in σ} e_i in R^n, and let P_M = conv{e_σ : σ is a basis of M}. Let Δ = {x in R^n : x_i ≥ 0 for all i and x_1+...+x_n = r}. If rank(F) denotes the matroid rank of a flat F ⊆ [n], then P_M is exactly the set of all x in Δ satisfying sum_{i in F} x_i ≤ rank(F) for every flat F of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Every facet inequality of P_M may be written as sum a_i x_i ≤ b. Since every edge of a matroid polytope is parallel to some e_i − e_j, the normal vector of a facet can only impose equalities a_i = a_j along edge directions lying in that facet. After adding a multiple of the affine equation sum x_i = r and scaling, the normal vector can be assumed to be a 0/1 vector, hence the inequality has the form sum_{i in G} x_i ≤ b_G for some subset G of [n]. The optimal right side over basis vectors is max{|σ ∩ G| : σ basis of M}, which equals rank(G), because every independent subset of G can be extended to a basis of M. If F is the flat spanned by G, then G ⊆ F and rank(G) = rank(F), so the flat inequality for F implies the inequality for G. Therefore the inequalities indexed by flats, together with the simplex equations and nonnegativity, cut out exactly P_M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a matroid M on [n], define two elements i,j in [n] to be equivalent if there is a circuit C of M with {i,j} ⊆ C. The equivalence classes are the connected components of M, and c(M) denotes their number. Then the dimension of the matroid polytope P_M is n − c(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The affine span of P_M is generated by differences e_σ − e_τ between basis indicator vectors. Such differences are spanned by elementary basis-exchange directions e_i − e_j, where i and j can be exchanged between two bases. The elements i and j are exchange-connected in this sense exactly when they lie in a common circuit, i.e. when they are in the same connected component of M. Thus the linear space parallel to aff(P_M) is the direct sum, over connected components, of the hyperplanes in the coordinate subspaces of those components whose coordinate sums are zero. Its dimension is the sum over components C of |C|−1, namely n−c(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a rank r matroid on [n]. For w in R^n, let M_w be the matroid whose bases are the bases σ of M maximizing the w-weight sum_{i in σ} w_i, and let P_M^w be the face of P_M on which the linear functional x ↦ sum_i w_i x_i is maximized. The following are equivalent: (1) w lies in the Bergman fan B̃(M), meaning that for every circuit C of M the minimum of {w_i : i in C} is attained at least twice; (2) M_w has no loops; (3) every element i in [n] lies in some basis of M_w; (4) P_M^w has nonempty intersection with the relative interior of the simplex Δ = {x_i ≥ 0, sum x_i = r}; (5) the functional sum_i w_i x_i attains its maximum over P_M at a point of ∂P_M \ ∂Δ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Conditions (2) and (3) are equivalent by the definition of a loop in a matroid: an element is a loop precisely when it belongs to no basis. Conditions (3) and (4) are equivalent because P_M^w is the convex hull of the indicator vectors of the bases of M_w; this convex hull contains a point with all coordinates positive exactly when the union of those bases is all of [n]. Conditions (4) and (5) are the same geometric assertion, since P_M^w is the maximum face and points in the interior of Δ avoid the boundary of the ambient simplex. For the equivalence with (1), if the Bergman circuit condition fails, then there is a circuit C and an element i in C with w_i strictly smaller than all w_j for j in C\{i}. Whenever a basis contains i, circuit exchange along C replaces i by some j of larger w-weight, so no maximum-weight basis contains i; hence i is a loop of M_w. Conversely, if i is a loop of M_w, the paper uses the standard matroid exchange/circuit characterization to produce a circuit C containing i such that w_i is strictly smaller than the other weights on C, so the Bergman condition fails.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume M is a connected matroid on [n]. Let F be a flat of rank s. Define the restriction M[∅,F] to be the rank s matroid with bases σ ∩ F, where σ runs over bases of M with |σ ∩ F| = s. Define the contraction M[F,[n]] to be the rank r−s matroid with bases σ\F, where σ runs over bases σ of M. Let P_M^F be the face of P_M on which the flat inequality sum_{i in F} x_i ≤ rank(F) is tight. Then the matroid associated to this face is M_F = M[∅,F] ⊕ M[F,[n]] = {σ basis of M : |σ ∩ F| = s}. The flat F is a flacet, meaning that P_M^F is a facet of P_M, if and only if both M[∅,F] and M[F,[n]] are connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality sum_{i in F} x_i = s selects precisely those basis vectors e_σ for which σ contains a basis of F, equivalently |σ ∩ F| = s. Such a basis decomposes uniquely into a basis of the restriction to F and a basis of the contraction by F, giving the direct-sum decomposition M_F = M[∅,F] ⊕ M[F,[n]]. Consequently P_M^F is the direct product P_{M[∅,F]} × P_{M[F,[n]]}. By Proposition 2.4 its dimension is |F| − c(M[∅,F]) plus n−|F| − c(M[F,[n]]). Since M is connected, P_M has dimension n−1, so P_M^F is a facet exactly when this sum is n−2. Both component counts are positive integers, so this happens exactly when both minors are connected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid and let P_M^* be the polar dual of its matroid polytope P_M, after quotienting by the line R(1,...,1) or equivalently intersecting the normal fan with the sphere S = {w in R^n : sum_i w_i = 0 and sum_i w_i^2 = 1}. For each face Γ of P_M^*, let M_Γ be the initial matroid M_w for any w in the corresponding relatively open cone of the normal fan of P_M. Then the Bergman complex B(M) is the subcomplex of ∂P_M^* consisting of all faces Γ such that M_Γ has no loop. If M is connected, the vertices of B(M) are indexed by the flacets of M. Moreover B(M) is homotopy equivalent to ∂P_M \ ∂Δ, where Δ is the ambient hypersimplex/simplex containing P_M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first assertion is a direct translation of Proposition 2.5: a normal cone belongs to the Bergman fan exactly when its associated initial matroid is loopless. Vertices of the dual polytope correspond to facets of P_M, and among the non-simplex boundary facets the relevant facet-defining flats are precisely the flacets, giving the vertex statement for connected M. The homotopy equivalence is the standard Alexander-duality-type relation between a subcomplex of the boundary of a polytope and the complementary co-complex in the boundary of the dual polytope; barycentric subdivision gives an explicit proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.1, 3.2, 3.5 and Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a finite lattice with bottom element 0 and top element 1. A subset G of L_{>0} is a building set if, for every X in L_{>0}, writing max G_{≤X} = {G_1,...,G_k}, there is a poset isomorphism product_j [0,G_j] ≅ [0,X] whose j-th factor embeds by the natural inclusion. If G contains 1, a subset S of G is nested if, whenever X_1,...,X_t are pairwise incomparable elements of S with t ≥ 2, their join X_1∨...∨X_t does not belong to G. The nested sets form an abstract simplicial complex Ñ(L,G), a cone with apex 1; its link is denoted N(L,G). If L is atomic with atoms A_1,...,A_n, each G in G has a characteristic vector v_G in R^n recording which atoms lie below G, and the cones R_{≥0}{v_G : G in S}, for nested sets S, form a simplicial fan Σ̃(L,G). Proposition 3.6 states: (1) for any atomic L and building set G, Σ̃(L,G) is unimodular; (2) if H ⊆ G are building sets in L, then Σ̃(L,G) is obtained from Σ̃(L,H) by a sequence of stellar subdivisions, and all such fans have the same support; (3) for any atomic L and building set G, N(L,G) is homeomorphic to the order complex Δ(L) of the proper part of L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes these as known results from Feichtner-Yuzvinsky and Feichtner-Müller rather than reproving them. The construction uses the factorization property of a building set to ensure that nested sets give linearly independent characteristic vectors and that cones meet along common faces. Enlarging a building set corresponds combinatorially to inserting additional building-set elements and geometrically to stellar subdivision at the corresponding ray. Since stellar subdivision preserves support and homeomorphism type, all nested set complexes for a fixed atomic lattice have the same support and topology as the order complex.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a family of subsets of [r]. Then F is a building set in the Boolean lattice 2^[r] if and only if F contains every singleton {i}, i in [r], and satisfies the closure condition: whenever F,F′ in F have F ∩ F′ nonempty, their union F ∪ F′ also lies in F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For X ⊆ [r], the set F_{≤X} consists of members of F contained in X. The product decomposition required in the definition of a building set says exactly that the maximal elements of F_{≤X} are pairwise disjoint and have union X. Having all singletons ensures the union condition for every X. The pairwise-disjointness of maximal elements is equivalent to saying that two overlapping members of F cannot both remain separate below their union; by repeatedly applying the condition F∩F′ nonempty implies F∪F′ in F, overlapping pieces merge into a larger member of F. This is precisely the Boolean building-set condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any family F of subsets of [r], there is a unique minimal enlargement bF of F that is a building set in the Boolean lattice 2^[r]. The paper calls bF the building closure of F. Explicitly, bF consists of all singletons together with all subsets X of [r] for which the simplicial complex with facets max F_{≤X} is connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If a building set contains F, Lemma 3.9 forces it to contain every union of overlapping members of F, iterated as necessary, and also every singleton. For a fixed X, such forced unions are exactly detected by connectedness of the simplicial complex whose facets are the maximal F-members contained in X. The family bF defined by these connected subsets satisfies the Boolean building-set overlap condition of Lemma 3.9, hence is itself a building set. Minimality and uniqueness follow because every building set containing F must contain all the subsets just described.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.12 and Corollary 3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a family F of subsets of [r], let Δ_F = {x in R^r : x_i ≥ 0, sum_i x_i = 1, and x_i = 0 for i notin F} be the face of the standard simplex supported on F, and let Δ_Family = sum_{F in F} Δ_F be their Minkowski sum. Then Δ_Family consists of all nonnegative x in R^r with sum_i x_i = |F| and, for every G ⊆ [r], satisfying sum_{i in G} x_i ≥ |{F in F : F ⊆ G}|. It is enough to impose these inequalities for G in the building closure bF. If [r] belongs to F, then Δ_Family has dimension r−1 and the irredundant facets are indexed by bF.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each summand Δ_F is a face of the standard simplex, so every edge of the Minkowski sum is parallel to some e_i−e_j; thus the polytope is a Minkowski summand of the permutohedron and is cut out by inequalities involving coordinate sums over subsets G. The minimum of sum_{i in G} x_i on the summand Δ_F is 1 exactly when F ⊆ G and is 0 otherwise. Summing over all F gives the stated lower bound. The face where the inequality for G is tight decomposes as the Minkowski sum of the simplices Δ_F for F ⊆ G plus the simplices Δ_{F\G} for F not contained in G. For such a face to be a facet, the subfamily inside G must be connected in the sense of the building closure. If [r] is itself a summand, the full polytope is (r−1)-dimensional, and the same face-dimension calculation shows that every G in bF gives an actual facet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.14
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a building set in the Boolean lattice 2^[r] and assume [r] belongs to F. Let Δ_Family = sum_{F in F} Δ_F be the Minkowski sum of the corresponding faces of the standard simplex. Then Δ_Family is an (r−1)-dimensional simple polytope. Its normal fan is a unimodular simplicial fan, and this fan is combinatorially isomorphic to the nested set complex N(2^[r],F).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since F is already its own building closure and contains [r], Corollary 3.13 gives an (r−1)-dimensional polytope whose facets are indexed by the proper members of F. Pick a generic linear functional w and relabel so w_1 < w_2 < ... < w_r. The vertex v minimizing w on Δ_Family has coordinate v_i equal to the number of sets F in F whose smallest element is i. For a building-set member G, the facet inequality indexed by G is tight at v exactly when every F whose w-minimal element lies in G is actually contained in G. This happens precisely for the sets G_i = union{F in F : min(F)=i}. The building-set property ensures each G_i lies in F. Since G_1 = [r], the proper tight facets at v are G_2,...,G_r, exactly r−1 of them, so v is simple; by relabeling, every vertex is simple. These tight-facet collections are exactly the maximal nested sets, obtained recursively by splitting into maximal proper nested pieces. Finally, the edge directions are among the roots e_i−e_j, a unimodular configuration, so the normal fan is unimodular.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.15
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any family F of subsets of [r] with building closure bF, the normal fan of Δ_bF triangulates the normal fan of Δ_F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since F ⊆ bF, the Minkowski sum Δ_F is a Minkowski summand of Δ_bF. For polytopes, taking a larger Minkowski sum refines the normal fan. Theorem 3.14 says that the normal fan of Δ_bF is simplicial when bF is a building set, so this refinement is a triangulation of the possibly nonsimplicial normal fan of Δ_F.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F be a building set in the Boolean lattice 2^[r] with [r] in F. An F-tree is a rooted tree whose nodes ν are labelled by nonempty, pairwise disjoint subsets T_ν of [r], such that for each non-root node ν the union T_{≤ν} of all labels in the subtree below ν belongs to F. For a tree T, let sets(T) = {T_{≤ν} : ν is a non-root node}. Then for every nested set S of F there is a unique F-tree T such that sets(T)=S, and the nested set complex is exactly {sets(T) : T is an F-tree}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Given an F-tree, the nonempty differences T_ν = T_{≤ν} \ T_{<ν} force the associated subsets to satisfy the nestedness condition: incomparable subtree unions cannot have a join lying in F without contradicting the tree decomposition. Conversely, starting from a nested set S, include [r] for the coned version and let G_2,...,G_k be the maximal proper elements of S. Their union cannot be all of [r] unless it violates nestedness, so the root receives the nonempty label [r]\(G_2∪...∪G_k). The restrictions of F and S to each G_i again form a Boolean building-set/nested-set pair, so the construction recurses inside each G_i. This gives existence, and the maximal proper elements at each recursive step are forced, giving uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be any matroid and let G be a building set in its lattice of flats L_M, with the nested-set conventions of Section 3. Then the nested set fan Σ̃(L_M,G) refines the Bergman fan B̃(M). Equivalently, after quotienting by the line R(1,...,1) and intersecting with the unit sphere, the geometric realization Σ(L_M,G) of the nested set complex N(L_M,G) is a triangulation of the Bergman complex B(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces to basis charts. For a basis σ of M, consider the local Bergman complex B_σ(M), the intersection of B(M) with the facet of the dual matroid polytope corresponding to σ. Theorem 4.2 identifies B_σ(M) with the normal fan of a Minkowski sum of simplices determined by the fundamental circuits of elements outside σ, and identifies the localized nested set complex N(L_M(σ),G_σ) with a simplicial refinement of that normal fan. Now take any point w on the sphere and any basis σ of the initial matroid M_w. Then w lies in the support of the global nested set complex if and only if it lies in the support of the localized nested set complex for σ; by Theorem 4.2 this is equivalent to lying in the local Bergman complex for σ; and this is equivalent to w lying in B(M). Gluing these local triangulations over all bases gives the global refinement.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume M is a connected rank r matroid and σ is a basis of M. Let L_M(σ) be the sublattice of flats of M that are spanned by subsets of σ; it is a Boolean lattice of rank r. For a building set G in L_M, let G_σ be the flats in G that are spanned by subsets of σ. Let B_σ(M) be the local Bergman complex obtained by intersecting B(M) with the facet of P_M^* dual to the vertex e_σ of P_M. Then the localized nested set complex N(L_M(σ),G_σ) triangulates B_σ(M). Both complexes are homeomorphic to an (r−2)-sphere, and each is naturally realized as the boundary complex of an (r−1)-dimensional polytope.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Relabel so σ = {1,...,r}. For each i outside σ, let F_i ⊆ σ be the subset such that F_i ∪ {i} is the fundamental circuit of i with respect to σ. If w lies in the local Bergman fan where σ is a maximum-weight basis of M_w, then basis exchange gives w_i ≤ w_j for all j in F_i, and the Bergman circuit condition on F_i∪{i} forces w_i = min{w_j : j in F_i}. Thus the whole local Bergman fan is parametrized by the piecewise-linear map ψ : R^r → R^n sending (w_1,...,w_r) to (w_1,...,w_r,w_{r+1},...,w_n), with w_i = min_{j in F_i} w_j for i>r. The domains of linearity of the coordinate min function for F_i are the cones of the normal fan of the simplex Δ_{F_i}; the common refinement is the normal fan of the Minkowski sum Δ_F = sum_{i>r} Δ_{F_i}. Therefore B_σ(M) is combinatorially the normal fan of Δ_F. Under the identification L_M(σ) ≅ 2^σ, the localized building set G_σ contains the building closure of the family {F_i}. By Theorem 3.14 and Corollary 3.15, the normal fan associated with G_σ is a unimodular simplicial refinement of the normal fan of Δ_F. This is the desired triangulation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any matroid M of rank r, the Bergman complex B(M) is homeomorphic to the order complex Δ(L_M) of the proper part of the lattice of flats L_M. Consequently B(M) is homotopy equivalent to a wedge of spheres of dimension r−2, and the number of spheres is the absolute value of the Möbius function of L_M from bottom to top.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 4.1 with the maximal building set G = L_M. For this building set, nested sets are exactly chains, so the nested set complex is the order complex of L_M. The homotopy type of the order complex of a geometric lattice is the classical one: a wedge of top-dimensional spheres, with the number of spheres given by the absolute value of the Möbius invariant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a matroid, G a building set in L_M, and S a nested set. Let M_S = M_w for any vector w in the relative interior of the cone/simplex corresponding to S in the nested set fan. Choose a basis σ such that S lies in the localized nested set complex N(L_M(σ),G_σ), represent S by the corresponding labelled G_σ-tree T, and for each node ν define flats F_{≤ν} = span(T_{≤ν}) and F_{<ν} = span(T_{<ν}) in L_M. Replace the label of ν by the interval [F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}] of L_M; the resulting tree T_S is independent of the choice of σ. If M[F,G] denotes the interval matroid/minor associated to the interval [F,G] of flats, then M_S = direct sum over nodes ν of T_S of M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A basis σ of M is a basis of the initial matroid M_S precisely when the cone for S lies in the local normal cone at σ, equivalently when S belongs to N(L_M(σ),G_σ). For such a σ, the tree T representing S has node labels T_ν that partition σ in a way compatible with the nested flats. For each node ν, T_ν is a basis of the interval matroid M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}], so σ is a basis of the direct sum of all these interval matroids. Conversely, if σ is a basis of that direct sum, then for each ν the set (σ ∩ F_{≤ν})\F_{<ν} is a basis of M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}]. Relabelling the same tree by these sets gives a G_σ-tree representing S, so S lies in the σ-local nested complex and σ is a basis of M_S. Since the two matroids have exactly the same bases, they are equal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With the conventions of the nested set fan, facets of the nested set complex N(L_M,G) are represented by partitions of the ground set [n] into r nonempty parts, where r is the rank of M. More explicitly, for a maximal nested cone, the tree decomposition in Theorem 4.4 has r rank-one interval matroids, and the corresponding subsets F_{≤ν}\F_{<ν} form a partition of [n].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a maximal nested set, the associated tree has r relevant nodes, and each interval matroid M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}] has rank one. Since the direct sum in Theorem 4.4 is loopless and has total ground set [n], the ground sets F_{≤ν}\F_{<ν} of these rank-one summands are nonempty and disjoint and cover [n]. Thus each maximal cone/facet determines such an unordered r-block partition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 5.1 and Corollary 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a connected matroid M, let N(M) denote the nested set complex for the minimal building set G_min of L_M, whose elements are the connected flats. Every flacet is a connected flat, but a connected flat need not be a flacet. The Bergman complex B(M) is triangulated by N(M) without adding vertices if and only if every connected flat of M is a flacet.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The vertices of B(M), by Theorem 2.7, are indexed by flacets. The vertices of the minimal nested set complex are indexed by connected flats, by the definition of G_min. Proposition 2.6 implies a flacet must be connected, but examples in the paper show the converse can fail. Therefore the triangulation from Theorem 4.1 introduces no new vertices exactly when the two indexing sets coincide, i.e. when every connected flat is a flacet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a connected matroid, and let N(M) be the nested set complex for the minimal building set of connected flats in L_M. Then N(M) equals the Bergman complex B(M), rather than merely triangulating it, if and only if for every pair of flats F ⊂ G with G connected, the interval matroid M[F,G] is connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each simplex S of N(M), let Γ_S be the smallest face of B(M) containing it. Equality N(M)=B(M) is equivalent to dim S = dim Γ_S for every S. The simplex has dimension |S|−1. The Bergman face Γ_S corresponds to the initial matroid M_S, and its dimension is c(M_S)−1, where c denotes the number of connected components. By Theorem 4.4, M_S is a direct sum over the interval matroids M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}] appearing as labels in the tree T_S. Thus c(M_S)=|S| exactly when every one of these interval matroids is connected. In such labels, F_{≤ν} is always a connected flat and F_{<ν} is a subflat, proving the forward implication from the stated interval-connectedness condition. Conversely, given any F⊂G with G connected, take the nested set consisting of G together with the connected components of F. In the associated tree, the root interval is [F,G]. If N(M)=B(M), the preceding dimension comparison forces M[F,G] to be connected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Algorithm 5.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper gives an algorithm for computing the maximal faces of the Bergman complex B(M) and the nested set triangulation N(M) of a rank r matroid M on [n], when M is given by its bases. The output represents maximal faces of B(M) by unordered partitions {B_1,...,B_r} of [n] into r nonempty blocks, as in Corollary 4.7, and for each such face records the maximal nested sets that subdivide it.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First precompute all connected flats of M. For each basis σ, compute for every i outside σ the fundamental-circuit subset F_i ⊆ σ such that F_i∪{i} is a circuit. For each ordering of σ, assign each outside element i to the first element of F_i in that ordering; together with the basis elements themselves this produces an r-block partition. Taking the union over all bases and all orderings gives the set Ω of partitions representing facets of B(M), because locally the Bergman fan is the normal fan of the Minkowski sum of the simplices Δ_{F_i}, and its vertices are obtained from generic orderings as in Theorem 3.14. For each partition ω in Ω, a connected flat F of rank s gives a nested-set vertex lying on the corresponding Bergman face exactly when F is the union of s blocks of ω. The collection Π of such flats is then used to list the maximal nested sets subdividing that face. If |Π|=r, the Bergman face is already an unsubdivided simplex; otherwise one computes the maximal nested subsets of Π.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 6.4 and Lemma 6.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume M is realized by an r-dimensional linear subspace V of C^n, or by X in P^{n−1}, and let X° = X ∩ (C*)^{n−1} be the hyperplane arrangement complement. Let I_X be the linear ideal of X. The map φ : X° → T_M, t ↦ t·I_X embeds X° into the projective toric variety T_M associated with the matroid polytope P_M, and the tropical compactification X_trop is the closure of φ(X°) in T_M. For a cone C in the normal fan of P_M and w in its relative interior, the following are equivalent: C is a cone of the Bergman fan B̃(M); the initial ideal in_w(I_X) contains no monomial; the initial ideal in_w(I_X) contains no variable. The paper then uses the standard tropical compactification orbit criterion that the cones whose torus orbits meet X_trop are precisely the cones satisfying this no-monomial condition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The torus orbit decomposition of T_M is the normal fan decomposition of P_M, and two weights w,w′ lie in the same cone exactly when they give the same initial ideal of I_X. Since I_X is generated by linear forms, an initial ideal contains a monomial if and only if it contains some variable. The matroid associated to the linear ideal in_w(I_X) is exactly the initial matroid M_w. Thus the condition that no variable lies in in_w(I_X) is the condition that M_w has no loop, which is equivalent to w being in the Bergman fan by Proposition 2.5. The final orbit-intersection statement is the general Hilbert-scheme/tropical compactification criterion: an initial degeneration is nonempty in the dense torus exactly when the corresponding initial ideal has no monomial. Note: in the supplied text, item (4) of Lemma 6.5 is printed with the words 'does not intersect', but the subsequent proof of Theorem 6.1 uses the standard 'intersects' formulation stated here.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X° be the complement of a hyperplane arrangement associated with a realizable connected matroid M. Let X_wond be the De Concini-Procesi wonderful compactification built from the minimal building set of connected flats, and let X_trop be the tropical compactification of X° in the toric variety associated with the matroid polytope P_M. Then there is a canonical morphism X_wond → X_trop. This morphism is an isomorphism whenever the following combinatorial condition holds: if a flat G in L_M corresponds to a divisor in X_wond, then G also corresponds to a divisor in the wonderful compactification of X ∩ F for every intersection of hyperplanes F containing G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each connected flat G, the projection to the coordinate projective space P_G gives the De Concini-Procesi map defining X_wond. Equivalently, X_wond is the closure of X° in the projective toric variety T_G associated with the Minkowski sum of simplices Δ_G over connected flats G. The matroid polytope P_M is a Minkowski summand of this Minkowski-sum polytope; this follows by comparing the flat inequality description of P_M from Proposition 2.3 with the inequality description of sums of simplices from Proposition 3.12. A Minkowski-summand relation gives a projective toric morphism T_G → T_M. The embedding of X° into T_M defining X_trop is the composition X° ⊂ T_G → T_M, hence taking closures gives a projective morphism X_wond → X_trop. The cones of the normal fan of the connected-flat Minkowski sum whose orbits meet X_wond form the minimal nested set fan N(M), while the cones of the normal fan of P_M whose orbits meet X_trop form the Bergman fan B(M). If the stated combinatorial condition holds, it translates to the interval-connectedness criterion of Theorem 5.3, so N(M)=B(M); in that case the toric morphism restricts to an isomorphism on the compactifications.

proof_techniques.name: Normal-fan interpretation of the Bergman condition
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly replaces the circuit definition of the Bergman fan by a statement about normal cones of the matroid polytope. A weight vector w determines an initial matroid M_w by selecting maximum-weight bases, and w lies in the Bergman fan exactly when M_w is loopless. Geometrically, this is equivalent to the exposed face P_M^w meeting the interior of the ambient simplex. This converts tropical linear conditions into polyhedral face and matroid-minor conditions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For questions about tropical linear subspaces contained in Trop(M), this gives a concrete way to test containment or boundary behavior by initial matroids and looplessness. It may help analyze any proposed order-reversing map on Dr(M) by checking how it acts on the initial matroids or normal cones associated with flats.

proof_techniques.name: Flat faces factor as restriction times contraction
proof_techniques.description: For a flat F, the face of P_M cut out by sum_{i in F} x_i = rank(F) has associated matroid M[∅,F] ⊕ M[F,[n]], the direct sum of the restriction to F and the contraction by F. The dimension calculation for this product proves that F defines a facet precisely when both minors are connected.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem's embedded copy of L(M)^op in Dr(M) is defined using contractions M/F together with loops on F. This paper's face-factorization is a precise polyhedral place where contractions and restrictions appear together, and it suggests studying a flat anti-involution through its effect on the two factors of such flat faces.

proof_techniques.name: Triangulating Bergman fans by nested sets of flats
proof_techniques.description: The main construction takes a building set G in the lattice of flats and forms a nested set fan from characteristic vectors of flats. Theorem 4.1 shows that this fan is a unimodular triangulation of the Bergman fan. Different building sets give different subdivisions, related by stellar subdivisions, with the same support.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution on the lattice of flats can be tested first on the combinatorial nested set models of Trop(M). If it sends nested configurations to nested configurations, or sends one building-set fan to another, it may provide a finite combinatorial scaffold for constructing or obstructing an involution on the relative Dressian.

proof_techniques.name: Local basis charts via fundamental circuits and Minkowski sums of simplices
proof_techniques.description: At a basis σ, the local Bergman fan is parametrized by the weights on σ; each outside element i has weight equal to the minimum over the fundamental-circuit subset F_i ⊆ σ. The domains of linearity are exactly the normal fan of the Minkowski sum of simplices sum_i Δ_{F_i}. The localized nested set fan is the normal fan of a larger Minkowski sum associated with a building closure, hence triangulates the local Bergman fan.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a local Boolean-lattice model for Trop(M). If the flat involution interacts well with bases or with fundamental circuits, one can try to define or disprove a global Φ by checking compatibility on these local Minkowski-sum fans.

proof_techniques.name: Tree representation of nested sets and interval-minor decomposition
proof_techniques.description: Nested sets in a Boolean localization are represented uniquely by rooted labelled trees. For a nested cone in the Bergman fan, Theorem 4.4 labels the nodes by intervals [F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}] in the lattice of flats and decomposes the corresponding initial matroid as the direct sum of interval matroids M[F_{<ν},F_{≤ν}].
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: An order-reversing involution F ↦ F^⊥ on L(M) naturally reverses intervals, sending [F,G] to something like [G^⊥,F^⊥]. The interval-minor decomposition suggests a concrete way to see whether a candidate involution on tropical subspaces could be defined by applying this reversal node-by-node to nested decompositions.

proof_techniques.name: Dimension comparison through connected components
proof_techniques.description: To decide when the minimal nested set complex equals the Bergman complex, the paper compares the dimension |S|−1 of a nested simplex with the dimension c(M_S)−1 of the Bergman face containing it. Theorem 4.4 then reduces equality to connectedness of all interval minors M[F,G] with G connected.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a useful obstruction-detection pattern: if an involution on a poset of tropical subspaces is expected to respect cells or dimensions, failures of connectedness in interval minors may create nonsimplicial cells or subdivisions that any extension of a flat-level involution must handle.

proof_techniques.name: Minkowski-summand toric morphisms
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 6.1 compares the matroid polytope P_M with a Minkowski sum of simplices indexed by connected flats. Because P_M is a Minkowski summand of that larger polytope, the corresponding normal fans refine and there is a projective toric morphism from the wonderful-model toric ambient space to the matroid-polytope toric ambient space. The nested set fan maps to the Bergman fan under this morphism.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If Dr(M) or a subposet of it can be realized through polyhedral or toric moduli data, an order-reversing involution might be studied via dual or opposite morphisms between polytopal normal fans. The summand/refinement method gives a way to convert combinatorial refinements into actual maps.

proof_techniques.name: Orbit-intersection criterion for tropical compactifications
proof_techniques.description: For a realizable matroid, the Bergman fan is identified with the set of cones in the normal fan of P_M whose initial ideals contain no monomial, equivalently whose torus orbits meet the tropical compactification. This bridges matroidal Bergman fan combinatorics and algebraic tropical compactification strata.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If tropical linear subspaces in Trop(M) are approached through realizations or initial degenerations, the no-monomial/no-loop criterion can be used to verify whether a candidate transformed object remains inside Trop(M).

other_useful_info: The paper does not discuss the relative Dressian Dr(M) or order-reversing involutions on it directly; its relevance is through the detailed structure of Trop(M) as a Bergman fan and through the role of flats, contractions, and interval minors. Example 2.8 gives a rank 4 matroid on 6 elements whose Bergman complex has square two-faces, showing that Bergman complexes need not be simplicial; the minimal nested set complex subdivides those squares. Examples 1.2, 3.4, and Remark 5.1 show that connected flats need not be flacets: for the graphic matroid of K4 with one edge removed, the Bergman complex is K_{3,3} while the minimal nested set complex subdivides one edge. Remark 5.4 notes that for the graphic matroid of the complete graph K_n, the nested set complex equals the Bergman complex and is the space of phylogenetic trees; the paper also cites Ardila-Reiner-Williams for equality of Bergman and nested set complexes for finite reflection arrangements. Example 5.7 treats the self-dual regular matroid R10 and computes its connected flats, nested tetrahedra, and Bergman facets; this may be a useful nontrivial test case for any proposed flat-duality extension. The paper cites Ardila-Klivans for the order-complex triangulation of Bergman complexes, Speyer for tropical linear spaces, and Tevelev for tropical compactifications.
why_relevant: Gives a detailed construction of Bergman fans from matroid polytopes and the lattice of flats. This is directly relevant to the embedding of flats into Dr(M) and to how contractions/localizations appear inside Trop(M).
